coredata.dc:title: Femtomolar detection of dopamine using surface plasmon resonance sensor based on chitosan/graphene quantum dots thin film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kamal Eddin, Faten Bashar
Fen, Yap Wing
Omar, Nur Alia Sheh
Liew, Josephine Ying Chyi
Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd
coredata.dc:description: Due to the crucial role of dopamine (DA) in health and peripheral nervous systems, it is particularly important to develop an efficient and accurate sensor to monitor and determine DA concentrations for diagnostic purposes and diseases prevention. Up to now, using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors in DA determination is very limited and its application still at the primary stage. In this work, a simple and ultra-sensitive SPR sensor was constructed for DA detection by preparation of chitosan- graphene quantum dots (CS-GQDs) thin film as the sensing layer. Other SPR measurements were conducted using different sensing layers; GQDs, CS for comparison. The proposed thin films were prepared by spin coating technique. The developed CS-GQDs thin film-based SPR sensor was successfully tested in DA concentration range from 0 fM to 1 pM. The designed SPR sensor showed outstanding performance in detecting DA sensitively (S = 0.011°/fM, R2 = 0.8174) with low detection limit of 1.0 fM has been achieved for the first time. The increased angular shift of SPR dip, narrow full width half maximum of the SPR curves, excellent signal-to-noise ratio and figure of merit, and a binding affinity constant (KA) of 2.962 PM−1 demonstrated the potential of this sensor to detect DA with high accuracy. Overall, it was concluded that the proposed sensor would serve as a valuable tool in clinical diagnostic for the serious neurological disorders. This in turns has a significant socio-economic impact.

head.ce:title: Femtomolar detection of dopamine using surface plasmon resonance sensor based on chitosan/graphene quantum dots thin film

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the crucial role of dopamine (DA) in health and peripheral nervous systems, it is particularly important to develop an efficient and accurate sensor to monitor and determine DA concentrations for diagnostic purposes and diseases prevention. Up to now, using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors in DA determination is very limited and its application still at the primary stage. In this work, a simple and ultra-sensitive SPR sensor was constructed for DA detection by preparation of chitosan- graphene quantum dots (CS-GQDs) thin film as the sensing layer. Other SPR measurements were conducted using different sensing layers; GQDs, CS for comparison. The proposed thin films were prepared by spin coating technique. The developed CS-GQDs thin film-based SPR sensor was successfully tested in DA concentration range from 0 fM to 1 pM. The designed SPR sensor showed outstanding performance in detecting DA sensitively (S = 0.011°/fM, R2 = 0.8174) with low detection limit of 1.0 fM has been achieved for the first time. The increased angular shift of SPR dip, narrow full width half maximum of the SPR curves, excellent signal-to-noise ratio and figure of merit, and a binding affinity constant (KA) of 2.962 PM−1 demonstrated the potential of this sensor to detect DA with high accuracy. Overall, it was concluded that the proposed sensor would serve as a valuable tool in clinical diagnostic for the serious neurological disorders. This in turns has a significant socio-economic impact.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a monoamine neurotransmitter (NT) with the chemical formula 3,4-dihydroxyphenethylamine. The importance of this catecholamine NT is due to its vital functions in the mammalian nervous system as it transfers the brain messages in a nerve impulse form. In the brain, only few and very specific regions can produce DA such as at the substantia nigra and the ventral tegmental area [1]. DA controls the central nervous system function and the physiological activities including; emotions, stress, mental cognition, attention and motivation, learning and memory [2]. Also, it plays critical roles in the hormonal, renal, and cardiovascular systems. DA physiological levels vary in the different biofluids of human body. In the blood, its normal concentration is less than 130 pM. While in the urine and cerebrospinal fluid, DA levels are 5 nM [3]. DA deficiency in human body will result in serious neurodegenerative diseases including Parkinson’s disease (PD) [4,5], Alzheimer’s disease [6], schizophrenia [7,8], and depression [9]. Thus, there is a dire need for highly sensitive and selective sensors to monitor DA levels and make meaningful measurements in fast time. The construction and development of these sensors for physiological investigations would be a vitally important diagnostic marker.

As a consequence, in recent years in light of DA significance and its determination in biological fluids, huge efforts deployed and many strategies have been reported including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–12], capillary electrophoresis [13–15], mass spectroscopy [16–17], microdialysis techniques [18], Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy [19], flow injection [20], enzymatic methods [21], electrochemical (EC) methods [22–24]. Aptamer electrodes also have the potential for the selective analysis of DA in real time. RNA aptamer sequence is the only available sequence that has the capability for DA specific binding. Many studies showed that electrostatic interactions between the sugar-phosphate backbone of RNA and the amine group of DA are involved in DA-RNA binding [25]. The employment of this method to analyse DA needs deep understanding of the RNA and the nuclease mapping [26–30]. Many optical methods such as colorimetry and spectrophotometry [31], fluorescence spectrometry [32,33], ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry [34], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [35], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [36–41], chemiluminescence (CL) [42], photoelectrochemical (PEC) [43–50], photoluminescence (PL) [51], solid phase spectrophotometry (SPS) [52] and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) [53] have been used for DA detection. For interested readers, more studies reported on the electrochemical and optical methods for DA detection were included in the recently published review paper [54].

Each method has its own favourable features and disadvantages. Most of them have some drawbacks which prevent these methods from meeting the increasing requirements for accurate, simple, and more efficient sensing. There is still need to make further improvements to overcome these challenges and develop DA biosensors.

Today, compared with other described methods, biosensors that based on surface plasmon resonance (SPR) belong to the most advanced technologies that filled the need for more meaningful information on bio-molecular interactions [55]. This high-sensitivity optical sensor has been developed in several configurations and has proven effective in the detection of biological analytes and medical diagnostics [56–60], food safety [61] and detection of environmental pollutants [62–66]. This powerful technique with its real-time analytical capabilities has received huge attention during the last two decades. However, the detection of NTs especially DA using SPR biosensors is still growing and the reported results until now are promising [3,67–78].

SPR is an optical process in which the incident beam of monochromatic and p-polarized light strikes the surface of the metal and interacts with its free electrons [79,80]. This label-free detection method is excellent in monitoring the changes of refractive index (RI) associated with the capture and adsorption of analyte molecules on the metallic sensor surface which causes shifting of SPR signal. This significant shift in SPR dip provides information on the biomolecular interaction and allows kinetics of the binding process determination. All available configurations to excite surface plasmons in SPR sensors share the same intrinsic phenomenon. However, SPR sensors based on prisms show higher sensitivity than others based on grating couplers. Additionally, they provide the best resolution when wavelength interrogation is used [81].

SPR biosensor has proven its efficiency in comparison with the conventional diagnostic tools due to its important advantages of high specificity and sensitivity, cost- effectiveness, real-time sensing capability, no requirement of labelling, and easy preparation and control. However, using this sensor to detect analytes of low molecular weights or in low concentrations still need sensitivity enhancement. Nanomaterials have demonstrated their great potential in sensing [82,83], batteries [84,85], electronics, medicine, and other fields due to their unique optical, magnetic, and electrical properties. The incorporation of nanomaterials with SPR biosensor was promising solution to improve its sensitivity. Therefore, more and more materials are needed to modify the SPR chip for DA detection. Graphene is a single layer of graphite that has unique physicochemical properties [86,87]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a special member of carbon-based nanomaterials and demonstrated several attractive properties such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, photostability, and a relatively high quantum yield compared with other carbon nanomaterials. DA detection with GQDs was reported using EC [88,102], PEC [43], and fluorescence-based sensors [89–93]. Nevertheless, GQDs issues related to the sensitivity and selectivity still a main challenge in the sensor applications field. Chitosan (CS) is a biodegradable and high molecular biopolymer with many amine groups. It was incorporated with other materials to enhance the selectivity of DA sensors [88,94–99]. To the best of our knowledge, the modification of gold layer with CS-GQDs composite film for DA detection using surface plasmon resonance sensor has not applied yet. Therefore, in this study, CS-GQDs as a sensing layer will be incorporated with SPR technique for the first time for the ultrasensitive determination of DA.

Graphene quantum dots GQDs (1 mg/mL), dopamine hydrochloride, chitosan medium molecular weight (MMW), and acetic acid (assay ≥ 99.7%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water (DW) was used throughout the experiment.

In order to prepare chitosan solution, 400 mg of MMW chitosan was dissolved with 50 mL of the 1% acetic acid which was subsequently stirred using a magnetic stirrer. It was allowed to stand overnight at room temperature until a clear solution was obtained [100]. Then, 1 mL of GQDs was added into 5 mL of chitosan solution and stirred for 1 h until it formed the required CS-GQDs solution. Depending on the dilution formula (M1V1 = M2V2), DW was used to dilute DA solution with a concentration of 1 M to produce DA solutions with concentration of 100 mM, 10 mM, 1 mM, 100 µM, 10 µM, 1 µM, 100 nM, 10 nM, 1 nM, 100 pM, 10 pM, 1 pM, 100 fM, 10 fM, and 1fM.

Substrates (24 mm × 24 mm × 0.1 mm) of glass were purchased from Menzel-Glaser, Germany. Above all, acetone was used to clean the substrates sufficiently and remove dust and fingerprint on their surfaces. Before the preparation of the sensing layers, the deposition of gold thin film with thickness of 50 nm was done on the glass substrate using SC7640 Sputter Coater for 67 s. Then, spin coating technique was used to disperse the CS, GQDS and CS-GQDs mixture uniformly on top of the gold layers. For CS thin film, about 0.5 mL of CS solution was placed to cover almost the Au layer surface of the glass substrate, then it was spun at 2000 rpm for 30 s using spin coater P-6708D. GQDs thin film was prepared using 0.5 mL of GQDs with the same steps. After that, 0.5 mL of CS- CS-GQDs mixture was spun at 2000 rpm for 30 s and CS-GQDs thin film was deposited.

To test the ability of CS, GQDs and CS-GQDs thin films to detect DA based on SPR principle, an optical spectroscopy based on Kretschmann configuration has been built in the laboratory. This homemade SPR setup contains a He-Ne laser (632.8 nm, 5 mW), an optical chopper, a polarizer, a pinhole, a prism with high refractive index (1.77861), a rotating stage driven by a stepper motor, a photodetector, and lock-in amplifier. The SPR measurements were performed by recording the reflection intensity of the laser beam as a function of the angle of incident light. The uncoated side of the glass substrate was attached using index matching gel to one side of the prism, and a hollow cell containing DA solution was in contact with the surface of the sensing layer. Both, the thin film attached to the prism and the solution cell were mounted and fixed on the rotating stage to control the incidence angle of the laser beam.

After passing the laser incident light through the prism, the Au layer was illuminated through the prism. The laser light reflected without change in its intensity as long as the incidence angle was under the condition of total internal reflection (TIR). However, at a certain incidence angle larger than the critical angle, the light satisfies the resonance conditions and generates surface plasmon waves at the interface. At this point, the observed reflected light decreases sharply showing a dip in the intensity corresponding to the θSPR.

The laser light contacts the solution at the centre of the O-ring. After the adsorption of DA molecules on the sensing layer, the RI will be changed and the position of the SPR dip will be shifted. The intensity of reflected beam was detected using the photodiode and recorded in conjunction with the rotation angle, then the lock-in-amplifier was used to process it. The experiment was implemented under controlled temperature (27 °C) and was repeated separately for each DA concentration many times. Fig. 1 shows SPR setup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for DA on Au thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, SPR measurements were carried out for the gold thin layer in contact with DW that was injected into the cell as reference solution (0 fM). SPR curve showed the resonance angle at 53.01°. After that, the angular positions of the SPR dips were scanned and measured with increasing DA concentrations from 0 fM (DW) to 1000 fM (1 pM) as shown in Fig. 2 .

By comparing the reflectance resonance dip for all different concentrations of DA solution as shown in Fig. 3 , it is clear that no angular shift has occurred and the resonance angle for all concentrations of DA and DW are the same. This confirms there was not any change in RI of the sensing medium during the increase of DA levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for DA on Au/CS thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The SPR measurements to detect DA was continued using gold layer modified by CS thin film. To get the baseline signal, the injection of DW into the cell was done to keep it in contact with the sensing layer. Then, the SPR angle obtained from the reflectance resonance dip of Au/CS contacted DW was 53.84°. This baseline signal was used later to compare the results obtained with different concentration of DA solution. Then, different concentration of DA solution ranged from 1 fM to 1 M was injected into the hollow cell separately one by one for the measurements. Fig. 4 shows the curves of SPR signals for different levels of DA solution that was in contact with CS sensing layer.

The resonance angles for 1 fM and 1 pM of DA solution obtained from SPR curves were 53.84° which are exactly the same value obtained using DW. This observation is mainly due to the small amount of DA molecules that available in the solution and had the chance to be adsorbed on the surface of Au/CS thin film. By increasing the concentration of DA solution from 1 pM to 1 µM, the number of DA molecules that were attached to Au/CS surface incremented slightly. SPR shifts here were in opposite direction as shown in Fig. 4. Next, as the concentration of DA solution increases from 1 µM to 1 mM the SPR angle was shifted by 0.55° comparing with the baseline. This was the saturation state where the SPR shift was maximum. By increasing DA concentration to 1 M, no shifting in resonance angle occurred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for DA on Au/ GQDS thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of DA using SPR spectroscopy was continued after replacing Au/CS thin film with Au/GQDs thin film. SPR measurements firstly started using DW, then were carried out by injection of 1fM, 1 pM, 1 nM, 1 µM, 1 mM, and 1 M of DA solution one by one into the attached cell. As shown from the SPR curves in Fig. 5 , SPR angles were determined for all used concentrations. In case of DW in contact with Au/GQDs sensing layer, SPR angle was 53.84° which was the same value obtained for Au/CS thin film.

Next, by injecting DA solution with concentrations of 1 fM and 1 pM respectively, the resonance angle was 53.84° the same as the angle in case of DW. As the concentration of DA solution increased from 1 pM to 1 nM, there was small shift in the SPR angle to the left. This is due to some degradation on the surface of GQDs thin film upon contact with DA solution. By increasing the DA concentration to 1 mM, the SPR shift still to left. Only the injection of the highest concentration (1 M) caused the significant SPR shift of 1.11° to the right.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for DA on Au/CS-GQDS thin film
ce:section.ce:para: In this stage, the SPR experiment was continued after replacing Au/GQDs thin film with Au/CS-GQDS thin film to detect DA. The measurements were carried out using DW and DA solution with different low concentrations of 1fM, 10 fM, 100 1fM, and 1 pM. The resonance angle for the used sensing layer that contacted the DW was 54.953° which is higher than the values obtained using the previous thin films. The DA solution that was injected with the concentration of 1 fM resulted in a resonance angle of 55.231° and shifted the SPR angle significantly by 0.278° from the SPR angle in the case of DW. Next, as DA concentration increased from 1 fM to 10 fM, the increment in the resonance angle from 55.231° to 55.512° was clear. By increasing the DA concentration from 10 fM to 100 fM, the SPR shift became 0.836° from the baseline. As the concentration of the DA solution increased to 1 pM, more DA molecules were adsorbed on the CS-GQDS thin film. Consequently, the RI was changed, and the change in the SPR angle was noticeable (0.837°) as shown in Fig. 6 .

After the modification of the gold layer with CS-GQDs nanocomposite, the proposed sensor had the capability to distinguish and differentiate the signal of the lowest concentration used reliably from its baseline signal when DW was used. The obtained results showed the ability of this sensor to detect 1fM of DA solution. To the best of our knowledge, it is the lowest detection limit obtained in DA sensing until now. Certainly, this was not a coincidence. This observation can be attributed to the employment of CS-GQDs thin film as a good modifier incorporated to SPR technique. To explain this, it is necessary to mention the complexation of DA with the functional groups on the surface of GQDs and the phenyl moiety of DA. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the GQDs enable noncovalent interaction with diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and strong π-π interaction due to the phenyl moiety of DA [88]. In addition to the mutual electrostatic attraction induced between negatively charged CS in these experimental conditions and positively charged DA [101]. These interactions increased the adsorption of DA molecules on the active layer and changed RI of the sensing layer. This in turns shifted the SPR angle significantly. Table 1 shows the comparison of the lowest detection limit of DA obtained by different sensors using CS, GQDs, and CS-GQDs on their own or in combination with other materials:

Generally, the SPR sensor performance should be analyzed in terms of two aspects; the sensitivity and the accuracy. So, there are several important parameters should be evaluated such as sensitivity, full width at half maximum (FWHM), detection accuracy, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and figure of merit (FOM).

The most important parameter to measure and evaluate the sensor sensitivity is the angular shift of resonance reflectance dip (Δθ) [103]. The Δθ can be calculated as the difference between the SPR angle of different levels of DA solution and the SPR angle of DW (0 M concentration). The sensitivity of SPR sensor can be defined as the ratio between the calculated Δθ and the measured concentration of the target. The shift of SPR angle for a given change in the RI of the sensing medium should be as large as possible to exhibit good sensitivity. The linear progression analysis of obtained results using Au/CS-GQDs thin film helped to determine and evaluate the sensor sensitivity as shown in Fig. 7 by considering the slope of this linear fit as the value of the sensor sensitivity. The fitting produced a slope of 0.011 with a correlation coefficient, R2 of 0.8174. This is an indicator that the SPR sensor chip modified with CS-GQDs thin film has the sensitivity of 0.011° fM−1.

SPR measurements also offer quantitative information on the strength of association and the tendency of dissociation. To evaluate the effectiveness of the interactions between the macromolecular, it is necessary to determine binding affinity constant [104]. The magnitude of the binding affinity depends mainly on the strength of the interactions between DA molecules and the active layer of the SPR sensor. To determine this constant, the angular shifts of the SPR dips (Δθ) were plotted as a function of the concentration of DA ranging from 0 fM to 1 pM. Then, they were fitted using Langmuir isotherm model as shown in Fig. 7. The equation of the used model is represented by [105]:
(1)

Δ θ

Where

is the maximum angular shift of SPR dip at saturation, C is the DA concentration, and

is the equilibrium dissociation constant.

The fitted graph gave an R2 value of 0.97 and
Δ θ
m a x
of approximately 0.827° which is about 0.01° lower than maximum angular shift of the SPR dip that was obtained experimentally, i.e.,0.837°. From the graph, the

value for DA on CS-GQDs thin film was 0.3375 fM. Thus, the affinity constant
K A
(reciprocal of

) calculated was 2.962 PM−1. The same analysis was also conducted to evaluate the interaction between the DA and Au layer where the
K D
value was 0.995 fM and
K A
was 1.004 PM−1 with R2 value of −0.33. The high value of
K A
in case of Au/CS-GQDs/DA-based SPR sensor indicates the high affinity DA molecules associates with CS-GQDs layer. While the high value of the dissociation constant
K D
in case of Au/DA -based SPR sensor shows low stability of interaction. The significant attraction of DA to CS-GQDs layer is probably due to the noncovalent interaction between the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the GQDs and diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA. The phenyl moiety of DA enables electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and strong π-π interaction. Also, negatively charged CS attracted electrostatically to the positively charged DA [88,101]. This combination of CS with GQDs in turns increased the adsorption of DA to the sensor chip and enhanced the sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The full width at half maximum of SPR signal and detection accuracy
ce:section.ce:para: For the accuracy evaluation, FWHM analysis was carried out. FWHM can be defined as the full angular width of the SPR curve at the half of the maximum value of the reflectance intensity [106,107]. It is assumed that the detection accuracy of the SPR sensor is inversely proportional to the FWHM of the SPR curve. It measures how accurately the resonance angle was determined from the SPR curve. The small value of FWHM indicates that the error in determining the SPR angle is minimum which in turn leads to accurate results [108]. Fig. 8 shows the detection accuracy and FWHM values from the SPR curve of Au/CS-GQDs thin film in contact with DW and other different levels of DA solution.

The FWHM value for Au/CS-GQDs thin film for DW was 2.714° with detection accuracy of 0.368 deg−1. Then, the FWHM value decreased to 2.705° for 1 fM of DA solution giving detection accuracy value of 0.369 deg−1. The values of FWHM obtained by the injection of DA solution with higher concentrations into the cell decreased. As shown in Fig. 8, the detection accuracy increased with the level of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal-to-noise ratio and figure of merit
ce:section.ce:para:
The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is another essential parameter to determine the sensor performance and quantify its precision. SNR combines the effects of resonance angle shift Δθ and the detection accuracy. It can be determined by multiplying them together.
(2)
S N R =
Δ θ
F W H M
= Δ θ × D e t e c t i o n A c c u r a c y

Clearly, the shape of the SNR curve shown in Fig. 8 mirrors the shift in the SPR angle shown in Fig. 6. This demonstrates that the effect of Δθ is greater than the effect of detection accuracy determining the SNR value. It is notable from Fig. 9 that increasing DA concentration led to noise reduction of the SPR signals which increased the SNR values for the proposed sensor. This is expected and it has been explained in the previous paragraphs, increasing the concentration of DA solution changed the RI of the sensing layer and increased the shift in resonance angle. The dependence of the SNR mainly on resonant angle shift makes it an indicator of binding affinity [109].

The figure of merit (FOM) is useful parameter to verify and comprehensively analyze the performance of any SPR sensor. It is defined as the ratio of the sensor’s sensitivity to FWHM. For the best performance of the SPR sensor, FOM should be maximum. As shown in Fig. 8, the increasing of DA concentration decreased the values of FOM. This can be explained that when the level of DA in the solution was increased, the decrease in the sensitivity of the proposed sensor was faster than the decrease in the FWHM values. Table 2 summarizes the values of SPR angles, Δθ, FWHM, SNR, detection accuracy, and figure of merit for CS-GQDs sensing layer exposed to DA solution of different concentrations.

Doubtless, the employment of this good modifier (CS-GQDs) with SPR sensor for ultrasensitive determination of DA has been effective. The measurements were carried out many times to evaluate the sensing performance of the proposed structure towards DA. It showed quite good repeatability. Meanwhile, there was change in the sensor characteristics over time. This change in the stability, can be due to the degradation in the thin film after the exposure to DA solution for several hours. The lowest detection limits of other developed SPR sensors using different materials are summarized in Table 3 .

Briefly, chitosan- graphene quantum dots (CS-GQDs) thin film-based SPR sensor was developed for ultrasensitive and fast detection of DA for the first time. The obtained results demonstrated that the developed sensor has the potential to monitor and determine the angular shift of the SPR dip when detecting very low concentrations of DA down to 1 fM. The sensitivity of the developed sensor was 0.01◦/fM. The highest FOM value was obtained when detecting 1 fM of DA and corresponded to the minimum value of SNR. This nanotechnology-based SPR sensor represents a new possibility for the rapid, easy, low cost and reliable diagnosis of DA deficiency.
